title: TPU v1 · A Wide, Multi-Bank 24 MiB Unified Buffer Feeds the Array
oneLine: The central memory-microarchitecture question is not only capacity but how much data can be delivered in parallel each cycle. Feeding all 256 systolic-array rows simultaneously requires Unified Buffer to be banked and read through a very wide port—a textbook case of deriving port and bank counts from peak concurrency.

legend.name: 24 MiB Unified Buffer
legend.desc: A large software-managed on-chip scratchpad rather than a hardware cache, with no tags or hit checks. Activations, intermediates, and temporaries stay here to minimize accesses to off-chip DDR3, which provides only ~30 GB/s.

legend.name: Multi-bank organization
legend.desc: The 24 MiB store is divided into independent banks, each with its own read port. Reading a slice from every bank in the same cycle creates the aggregate width required by the array; bank count is derived from per-cycle parallel demand.

legend.name: Wide read port (256 rows/cycle)
legend.desc: The left edge of the systolic array has 256 row inputs, all 256 requiring activations every steady-state cycle. The port width is 256 rows × element width—far wider than a conventional SRAM port—so it must be assembled from multiple banks.

legend.name: 256×256 MXU array
legend.desc: The customer of the memory microarchitecture. Its demand for 256 rows each cycle without starvation directly defines Unified Buffer port and bandwidth requirements, turning architecture-level peak compute into a hard memory constraint.

legend.name: 4 MiB accumulator
legend.desc: Each array column emits a partial sum at the bottom. The 4 MiB 32-bit accumulator must accept all columns concurrently and support repeated accumulation across K tiles without overflow; it is the wide-port counterpart on the write side.

steps.cap: ① Move weights and activations from off-chip DDR3 into the 24 MiB Unified Buffer. DDR3 is slow and narrow (~30 GB/s), so transfer enough once and circulate data on chip afterward.
② Divide UB into multiple banks and distribute addresses across them. During a read, the banks each return one slice in the same cycle, providing the physical basis of a wide port.
③ Concatenate bank outputs into an ultra-wide read port that feeds 256 activation rows to the MXU’s 256 left-edge inputs in one cycle, preventing array starvation.
④ Each array column emits a partial sum into the 4 MiB accumulator. For K-dimension tiling, partial sums accumulate repeatedly and become final only after the last pass.
⑤ After activation or normalization, write accumulated results back to Unified Buffer for the next layer. Avoiding DDR3 keeps bandwidth pressure on chip.

before: Derive specifications from the customer: The memory serves MXU. MXU consumes 256 activation rows each cycle and produces a full column of partial sums at its bottom; these rates directly set peak read and write widths and are the starting point for every bank and port decision.

Decisions to make: Total UB capacity (24 MiB), bank count, bank width, read/write port counts, accumulator capacity (4 MiB) and precision (32-bit), and address-to-bank mapping that avoids same-cycle conflicts.

Required inputs: Peak compute (~92 TOPS) and target utilization from architecture (③), off-chip bandwidth (DDR3 ~30 GB/s), target workload layer sizes and reuse, and area/power limits (28 nm, die ≤331 mm², 28–40 W).

Balance the design triangle: Capacity, bandwidth, and area compete. More capacity reduces DDR3 traffic but 24 MiB of SRAM already consumes substantial area; more width requires additional banks, decoding, and wiring. First satisfy the bandwidth floor needed to feed the array, then balance capacity against area.
during: Build width from banks rather than one exotic port: A single SRAM macro cannot provide a 256-row-per-cycle port. Split UB into banks with conventional ports, read them in parallel, and concatenate their outputs. Bank count is approximately required width ÷ per-bank width—the direct derivation from peak concurrency.

Map addresses to avoid bank conflicts: Row and column activation access patterns must distribute all 256 same-cycle values across distinct banks. If two requests collide in one bank, one waits and the array stalls. Interleaving granularity is therefore a critical design choice.

Size read and write sides separately: Reads feed 256 activation rows per cycle, while writes receive accumulator results and next-layer data. Their concurrency, width, and timing differ, motivating multiple ports or separate read/write banks to avoid contention.

Use capacity to keep data on chip: The 24 MiB target should hold the active layer’s activations and reusable data so ~30 GB/s DDR3 handles mainly initial load and final store. Capacity and tiling are coupled: oversized tiles do not fit, while undersized tiles reduce reuse and increase DDR traffic.

Specify the accumulator independently: The 4 MiB 32-bit accumulator accepts one partial sum from every column each cycle and preserves precision across repeated K-tile accumulation. INT8 (8-bit) multiply with INT32 (32-bit) accumulation is standard fixed-point inference practice.

Trade software management for simpler hardware: TPU uses an explicitly managed scratchpad rather than a cache, removing tag arrays, hit logic, and replacement control. Area and power serve storage and MACs instead, while XLA must schedule every transfer correctly and deterministically.

result.label: Organization
result.value: Parallel multi-bank structure forming a wide read port

result.label: Read-port target
result.value: Feed all 256 array rows every cycle; never starve

result.label: Bank / port count
result.value: Derived from peak concurrency (not published bit-for-bit for v1)

result.label: Accumulator
result.value: 4 MiB · 32-bit, accepting every column partial sum

after: Validate bandwidth and utilization: Use a cycle model to confirm the UB port feeds all 256 rows every cycle for target layers and meets array-utilization goals. If bank conflicts or insufficient width cause starvation, add banks, change interleaving, or retile.
Verify conflict-free address mapping: Simulate bank conflicts for row-wise, column-wise, and cross-tile access patterns. Explicitly cover tail tiles and dimensions not divisible by the array size.

Validate accumulator capacity and precision: Confirm 32-bit values do not overflow along the deepest K-accumulation chain and 4 MiB holds all concurrently active output columns; adjust maximum tile size if required.

Hand off to RTL (⑤) and memory IP: Implement bank selection, read/write ports, arbitration/interleaving, and the accumulator in synthesizable RTL, and instantiate matching SRAM compiler macros while checking die-area limits.

Reserve low-power hooks: Clock-gate or retain banks that are not accessed in a cycle. Deterministic software-managed access timing makes enables statically schedulable; this is standard inference-ASIC practice, not a disclosed v1 detail.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (internal UB bank count and port width are not published; combining banks into a wide port and deriving them from peak concurrency are common inference-ASIC practice)
